name: Charles Ives
abstract: Charles Edward Ives (; October 20, 1874 – May 19, 1954) was an American modernist composer, actuary and businessman. Ives was among the earliest renowned American composers to achieve recognition on a global scale.

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: But I also subscribe to what Charles Ives said. he said, “Prizes are for children.”

about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] You were doing Charles Ives. [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah I was.

about_quotes.speaker: Maria Schneider
about_quotes.evidence: there’s a lot of great composers that weren’t really discovered in their lifetime that much, the Charles Ives and people who went a great portion of their life being really unrecognized
about_quotes.doc_title: Maria Schneider interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Chuck Israels
about_quotes.evidence: I don’t care if you want to write a piece about if you’re Charles Ives and you want to write the Housatonic at Stockbridge I know Stockbridge and the Housatonic River very well
about_quotes.doc_title: Chuck Israels interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 11, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: The CEI is Charles Edward Ives and the CCP is Charles Christopher Parker. So, I don’t draw any lines when I write. I really draw on everything that’s available to me.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Roswell Rudd
about_quotes.evidence: we look back on the daddies of American music — Carl Ruggles, Charles Ives, Duke Ellington, names, and Charlie Parker — names that most people don’t even know
about_quotes.doc_title: Roswell Rudd interviewed by Monk Rowe, Ellenville, New York, June 23, 1998
